uddhared ātmanātmānaṁ nātmānam avasādayet |
ātmaiva hy ātmano bandhur ātmaiva ripur ātmanaḥ || 5 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: One must deliver himself with the help of his mind, and not degrade himself. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul, and his enemy as well.
One must deliver himself with the help of his mind, and not degrade himself. The mind is the friend of the conditioned soul, and his enemy as well.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1633 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The word ātmā denotes body, mind and soul – depending upon different circumstances. In the yoga system, the mind and the conditioned soul are especially important. Since the mind is the central point of yoga practice, ātmā refers here to the mind.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.5 By the self (Atman), i.e., by the mind, which is unattached to sense-objects, one should raise the self. One should not allow the self to sink by a mind which is of the contrary kind.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.5 Let one lift up oneself, raise oneself out of the ocean of the world, by oneself — by the mind dragged towards aspiration; let one not let oneself sink. For oneself alone is the friend of oneself; there is no other friend that can lift one out of saṁsāra; oneself alone is the enemy of oneself if engaged in the unrighteous.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.4 When this Yogin, because of his natural disposition to the experience of the self, loses attachment, i.e., gets detached from sense-objects, i.e., things other than the self, and actions associated with them — then he has abandoned all desires and is said to have climbed the heights of…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: 6.5 One should lift up oneself by oneself; one should not depress oneself. For oneself alone is one's friend, and oneself alone is one's enemy. The mind, when it engages with sense-objects, is the cause of bondage; the same mind, when it ceases such engagement, is the cause of liberation.

positions.rendering: the embodied/empirical self (the practitioner) — not yet the supreme Self at this stage
the discriminating self / mind (manas) — the same that engages with sense-objects can engage with the Self
the mind / conditioned soul — friend or enemy depending on whether it has been disciplined

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three traditions read ātman in 6.5 operationally rather than at full metaphysical scope. Shankara explicitly distinguishes this verse's ātman from the supreme Self of Advaita; Ramanuja reads it as the discriminating self; Prabhupada renders it as the mind. The doctrine of self-reliance (the practitioner is the only lifter of the practitioner) is identical across all three.

literal_meaning: Let one lift the self by the self; let one not let the self sink. The self alone is the friend of the self, and the self alone is the enemy of the self.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The most-quoted verse of the chapter. Krishna's central injunction: the self is the only lifter of the self. No external agent can do this work. The same self is both the friend and the enemy of the self — depending on whether it has been disciplined. Shankara reads ātman here as the embodied self, not the supreme Self; Ramanuja reads it the same way at this stage. The verse is operational, not metaphysical: the practitioner is the only one who can lift the practitioner.
